Version
  • Integration
  • Integration

Integration with VPL and PlantBiophysics

GreenhousesJL is designed as the climate engine in a modular greenhouse simulation stack, alongside VirtualPlantLab.jl for 3D plant architecture and PlantBiophysics.jl for leaf-level physiology.

Architecture overview

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Weather data (TMY)                    │
└────────────┬──────────────────────────────┬──────────────┘
             │                              │
             ▼                              ▼
┌────────────────────────┐    ┌────────────────────────────┐
│   GreenhousesJL        │    │   VirtualPlantLab.jl       │
│                        │    │                            │
│  • Cover, air, floor   │◄──►│  • 3D plant architecture   │
│  • Soil conduction     │    │  • Monte Carlo ray-tracing │
│  • Heating (PI)        │    │  • PAR per leaf            │
│  • Walls               │    │  • Dynamic growth (FSPM)   │
│                        │    │                            │
│  Outputs:              │    │  Outputs:                  │
│  T_air, T_cov, Q_heat  │    │  PAR_leaf, LAI, geometry   │
└────────────┬───────────┘    └──────────────┬─────────────┘
             │                               │
             │         ┌─────────────────────┘
             │         │
             ▼         ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         PlantBiophysics.jl                 │
│                                            │
│  • Photosynthesis: FvCB (Farquhar)         │
│  • Stomatal conductance: Medlyn (2011)     │
│  • Transpiration: Stanghellini (1987)      │
│  • Leaf energy balance                     │
│                                            │
│  Outputs:                                  │
│  A_n, g_s, T_leaf, transpiration           │
└────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Coupling variables

The three packages exchange data through a small set of physical variables:

VariableFromToUnitDescription
$T_{air}$GreenhousesJLPlantBiophysicsKIndoor air temperature
$PAR_{leaf}$VPLPlantBiophysicsµmol/m²/sLight on each leaf
$T_{leaf}$PlantBiophysicsGreenhousesJLKCanopy temperature
$\dot{m}_{v}$PlantBiophysicsGreenhousesJLkg/m²/sTranspiration flux
$A_n$PlantBiophysicsVPLµmol CO₂/m²/sNet assimilation
$LAI$VPLGreenhousesJLm²/m²Leaf area index
$I_{glob}$GreenhousesJLVPLW/m²Solar radiation at cover

Implementation plan

Step 1: Canopy node in GreenhousesJL (current work)

Add a canopy temperature node to the energy balance:

# Future extension of greenhouse_rhs!
dT_canopy = (Q_rad_sol_can + Q_cnv_air_can - Q_rad_can_cov - Q_transpiration) / C_canopy

where Q_transpiration = λ × MV_CanAir is the latent heat from Stanghellini transpiration computed by PlantBiophysics.

Step 2: VPL → GreenhousesJL coupling

VPL computes PAR interception per leaf via 3D ray-tracing. The canopy-level sum provides the PAR input to the simplified Vanthoor photosynthesis model in GreenhousesJL:

# In VPL simulation loop:
par_per_leaf = ray_trace(scene, solar_source)

# Aggregate to canopy level for GreenhousesJL:
PAR_canopy = sum(par_per_leaf .* leaf_areas) / A_floor

Step 3: PlantBiophysics leaf energy balance

For each leaf, PlantBiophysics computes:

using PlantBiophysics

# Leaf-level energy balance
eb = energy_balance(
    Monteith(),           # or Stanghellini() when implemented
    leaf;
    Ra_SW_f = par_leaf,   # shortwave absorbed
    T_air = T_air,        # from GreenhousesJL
    VPD = vpd,            # from GreenhousesJL (air humidity)
    Wind = u_air,         # indoor air speed
)

The aggregated transpiration and canopy temperature feed back into the GreenhousesJL energy balance.

Step 4: PV panel shading

VPL can model PV panels as opaque Lambertian surfaces in the 3D scene. The transmitted PAR through the PV structure is automatically computed by the ray-tracer, providing a physically consistent coupling between agrivoltaic design and crop productivity.

Why Julia for the full stack?

All three packages are written in Julia, enabling:

  • No inter-language overhead: direct function calls between packages, no serialisation;
  • Shared type system: VPL geometry, PlantBiophysics leaf models, and GreenhousesJL climate share the same Float64 arrays;
  • Composable AD: automatic differentiation through the full pipeline for gradient-based optimisation (e.g. optimal PV panel placement);
  • Speed: the combined simulation (climate + 3D light + physiology) runs fast enough for parametric studies and control optimisation.
« LibraryBibliography »
